title: Dirty Spaghetti
subtitle: Ground beef and turkey sausage in a rich, roux-thickened sauce with Cajun spice. One-pot or classic.

ingredients.heading: Ingredients
ingredients.note: The roux (flour step) is what makes it "dirty"—it thickens the sauce without jarred tomato sauce.
items: 1 lb ground beef
1 lb ground Italian turkey sausage
1 red bell pepper, diced
1 green bell pepper, diced
1 yellow onion, diced
4–6 cloves garlic (or 1 tbsp garlic paste)
2 tbsp tomato paste
4 tbsp all-purpose flour
3–4 cups beef broth
16 oz spaghetti noodles
½–1 cup pasta water (as needed)
1 tsp Nature's seasoning
1–2 tsp Cajun seasoning
1 tsp Italian seasoning
ingredients.callout: Key tip: Don't skip the flour step—cooking it into the fat creates the thick, glossy sauce that defines this dish.

body.html: Dirty Spaghetti is a Southern-style one-pot pasta where the sauce is built from scratch in the same pot as the meat. A flour roux gives it body, Cajun seasoning gives it depth, and the pasta absorbs everything as it cooks.

items: Brown beef and turkey sausage together
Build flavor base with peppers, onion, and garlic
Cook tomato paste and add seasonings
Make the roux—flour + fat thickens the sauce
Add beef broth and simmer until thick
Cook pasta right in the pot (or separately)

body.html: What makes it "dirty": The roux—flour cooked into the meat fat—thickens the broth into something rich and glossy. No jarred sauce needed.

items: In a large pot, combine ground beef and turkey sausage.
Cook over medium-high heat, breaking up the meat as it cooks.
Cook until fully browned with no pink remaining.
Drain excess grease if needed—leave a little behind for flavor.

body.html: Use a big pot: You'll be adding broth, pasta, and sauce later. Don't start in a skillet.

items: Add diced onion and bell peppers to the pot.
Cook 4–5 minutes until softened, stirring occasionally.
Stir in garlic and cook 1 minute—don't let it brown.
Add tomato paste and stir to coat the meat and vegetables.
Cook 2–3 minutes until the paste darkens slightly—this removes the raw tomato taste.
Season with Nature's, Cajun, and Italian seasoning. Stir well to combine.

body.html: Cook the paste: Letting tomato paste caramelize for a few minutes builds a deeper, richer base than adding it with the broth.

slides.title: Make the Roux
slides.checkboxLabel: Flour stirred in and cooked 1–2 min

items: Sprinkle flour evenly over the meat mixture.
Stir well to coat everything—no dry flour pockets.
Cook 1–2 minutes, stirring constantly, until the flour smells slightly nutty.

body.html: This is the "dirty" step. The flour-fat roux is what transforms beef broth into a thick, glossy sauce. Don't rush it.

slides.title: Create the Sauce
slides.checkboxLabel: Broth added, sauce simmered and thickened

items: Slowly pour in beef broth while stirring—adding gradually prevents lumps.
Scrape up any browned bits from the bottom of the pot.
Bring to a simmer and cook until the sauce thickens noticeably.

body.html: Target consistency: Sauce should coat a spoon. It'll loosen slightly when pasta is added, so err thicker rather than thinner.

slides.title: Cook the Pasta
slides.checkboxLabel: Pasta cooked and tossed in sauce

items: Add dry spaghetti directly into the pot (break in half if needed).
Cover and cook until tender, stirring occasionally to prevent sticking.
Add a splash of water to loosen the sauce if needed.
Boil spaghetti in salted water until al dente.
Reserve ½–1 cup pasta water before draining.
Add drained pasta to the meat sauce and toss well.
Add reserved pasta water as needed to bring everything together into a silky sauce.
